title: Missing synthetic monitoring for SECRET-tier classification boundary
reasoning: Secret-tier evidence (STRAP, MOD-involved trials) is air-gapped and cannot be monitored via RUM (no applicant access). Classification boundary rejects must be audited but synthetic tests cannot probe SECRET tier from unclassified network. Current monitoring plan relies on production audit logs only, missing real-time alerting on classification-service degradation affecting MOD users.
recommendation: Establish on-premises synthetic test running within air-gapped network (MOD-hosted workstation). Test monthly: (1) tier-transition from OFFICIAL to SECRET, (2) rejection of out-of-tier evidence, (3) latency <2s for classification decision. Coordinate with MOD ITSO for workstation access and test scheduling. Log synthetic test results to immutable audit trail (not accessible from unclassified side; share summary reports only).

title: No real-time alert correlation between classification-tier changes and regulator calibration drift
component: Classification boundary service + AI-flag narrative service (NFR-MON-WB-008, NFR-MON-WB-011)
reasoning: When classification library is updated (3-4 times/year per Ada capacity) or clause versioning changes, AI-flag generation may drift (different evidence retrieval logic, different semantic search results). Simultaneously, regulator override rates may spike because flags no longer align with their previous calibration. Current monitoring treats these as independent; no cross-correlation to identify root cause (library change) vs symptom (calibration drift).
recommendation: Implement alert correlation: if library revision advances AND regulator override rate increases >15% within 24 hours, trigger 'Possible library-change impact on AI flags' alert (confidence: medium). Include in alert: which clauses changed, override-rate magnitude, affected case types. Do NOT auto-remediate; escalate to CAA Innovation Hub for investigation. Add monthly report: library changes vs override-rate deltas (train regulator expectations on expected variance).

title: Missing distributed-trace instrumentation for end-to-end coverage matrix journey (applicant → matrix PDF)
reasoning: Noyce NFR-LAT-003 budgets 600s p95 for matrix generation (100 MB pack). Component budgets are: OCR 180s, semantic classification 200s, matrix computation 90s, PDF rendering 40s. Current APM monitoring tracks aggregate inference latency, but missing distributed-trace correlation IDs linking: upload request → evidence store fetch → OCR batch → semantic classifier → matrix computation → PDF renderer. When p95 exceeds 600s, cannot pinpoint which component(s) are over budget; optimization is blind.
recommendation: Inject OpenTelemetry trace IDs (W3C format) at evidence-upload initiation. Propagate through: storage fetch, OCR job submission, semantic inference queue, matrix DB query, PDF rendering. Instrument each stage with start/end timestamps and component-specific metrics (OCR: pages processed, semantic: tokens classified, PDF: elements rendered). Visualize end-to-end trace in distributed-trace UI (Jaeger/Datadog). Alert if any trace exceeds 600s; highlight slowest component in trace.

title: High-volume panel-action log write alerts (quorum replication latency spikes) may cause alert fatigue
reasoning: Audit-trail writes during high-concurrency panel deliberation (10+ reviewers, 30-50 actions/sec) experience quorum-replication latency spikes >500ms due to network jitter, fsync stalls, or GC pauses on Kafka broker. Current alert config: alert_critical if p95 > 500ms sustained for 10 minutes. In practice, cluster-wide GC pause might spike 15-20 writes in a row to >500ms, triggering 10-minute sustained alert → operations team gets paged for false-positive (system recovered after GC finished). Leads to alert fatigue and slower incident response.
recommendation: Refine alert thresholds: (1) warn if p95 > 500ms for 30+ consecutive seconds (short spikes are expected under load), (2) critical if p95 > 500ms for 10+ minutes AND error rate >0.1% (indicates structural problem, not transient GC). Exclude GC pause windows: detect GC events via JVM metrics, suppress alert during known GC phases. Additionally, implement alert aggregation: if >50 panel-action-write warnings in 1 hour, group into single 'High write-latency period' incident (prevent individual-message alerts). Provide runbook: 'Write latency spike > 500ms' → check Kafka broker CPU, check replication lag, check fsync errors.

title: Classification boundary reject rate alerts may spike during normal tier transitions (low signal-to-noise)
reasoning: Current alert: 'reject rate > 0.1% of access attempts triggers critical alert.' During normal operations, some rejects are expected (users misclick evidence tier, classification marking fails, etc.). When GCAP/Tempest trials ramp up (2026-2028), evidence volume increases, so absolute reject count grows even if percentage stays constant. Alert fatigue risk: if absolute rejects grow from 1-2 per day to 10-20 per day (legitimate growth), but alert fires on any spike >0.1%, operations team becomes desensitized.
recommendation: Establish baseline reject rate per trial phase: (1) Q2 2026: <0.05%, (2) Q4 2026: <0.1%, (3) Q4 2027: <0.15% (higher volume expected). Set alert thresholds dynamically based on forecast. Additionally, segment by reject reason: classify rejects as (a) expected (user in wrong tier), (b) suspicious (access-control bypass attempt), (c) data-quality (classification marking missing). Alert only on suspicious rejects (count any access attempt from OFFICIAL user to SECRET evidence). Generate daily report: reject counts by reason, no alert for expected baseline rejects.

title: C2 link packet-loss alerts will spike during legitimate RF stress tests and red-team exercises
component: C2 link SNR and packet loss (NFR-MON-SWM-002)
reasoning: Current alert: 'packet loss > 5% sustained for 10 seconds → critical.' Monthly red-team exercises intentionally degrade C2 link to test swarm resilience (RF jamming, spectrum interference). Similarly, authorized field trials in poor RF environments (maritime, mountains) will naturally experience higher packet loss. If alerts are not context-aware (don't know red-team window or trial location), every legitimate test will trigger false-positive criticality alerts, causing alert fatigue and slower response to real problems.
recommendation: Implement context-aware alerts: associate each trial window with expected RF conditions (baseline, degraded, or poor). Set alert thresholds relative to expected baseline: warn if loss rate > 1.5x baseline, critical if > 3x baseline (anomaly outside expectation). During red-team exercises, suppress loss-rate alerts (but keep SNR/jitter alerts active for safety). Tag all trial metadata with expected RF conditions; use as alert-suppression context. Generate post-trial report: actual vs expected packet loss, any anomalies outside RF envelope.

title: Missing CI/CD regression detection for coverage matrix generation (inference model changes)
reasoning: Coverage matrix engine (OCR + semantic classification) is ML-based. When model is retrained (monthly per adaptive training), new model may have higher latency (larger model) or different accuracy (may over/under-flag evidence). CI/CD deploys new model to staging; if p95 latency regresses >10% vs baseline, or if flag distribution shifts >20%, regression should be caught before production deployment. Current monitoring lacks 'build-over-build' regression test (compare staged model vs previous production model on held-out test set).
recommendation: Implement regression test in CI/CD pipeline: (1) on model update, compute p95 latency on fixed 100 MB test pack (Scotland case + SORA case per Ada benchmarks), (2) compare to previous model; fail if >10% latency regression, (3) compute flag distribution (% RED/AMBER/GREEN) on test set; fail if kappa <0.85 vs previous model (possible accuracy regression). Fail CI gate if regression detected; require explicit approval from ML/platform leads to override. Generate weekly report: model performance trend (latency, accuracy, calibration).

title: No canary-deployment monitoring for workbench feature rollouts (AI-flag narrative service)
reasoning: AI-flag narrative is long-running (30 min), high-stakes (regulator decisions depend on it). New LLM versions or prompt-tuning changes can alter output quality subtly (e.g., narrative becomes more verbose, flags shift from RED to AMBER). Current monitoring tracks latency and error rate, but missing quality metrics (narrative helpfulness, regulator trust). Rollout should be canary (5% traffic) with hold-back comparison: old vs new LLM on same evidence set, measure inter-rater agreement on which flags are better. Missing this, bad rollout reaches 100% production before quality regression detected.
recommendation: Implement canary rollout: (1) deploy new LLM to 5% of narrative requests for 24 hours, (2) hold-back comparison: run both old and new LLM on same evidence set (offline, not impacting regulator). Generate comparison report: latency delta, error-rate delta, narrative similarity (semantic comparison via embedding distance). Measure regulator satisfaction via survey on canary cohort (do they find new narrative more helpful?). Fail rollout if latency regresses >10%, or if satisfaction score <7/10 vs baseline. Proceed to 25% → 100% rollout in stages over 5 days.

title: Missing regression detection for clause library versioning (impact on readiness checks)
component: Clause library and readiness check (NFR-MON-WB-009, NFR-MON-WB-010)
reasoning: Clause library updates (3-4 major revisions/year per Ada capacity) change readiness validation rules. New clause may tighten acceptance criteria, causing historical pass-rate to drop (previously-passing submissions now fail). Without regression test, deployment of tightened clause library goes live → next day, 50% of submissions fail readiness check (spike in rejections) → applicants confused, support team overwhelmed. Regression should be caught pre-deployment: twin-sample test (Scotland case + SORA case) must maintain >95% pass rate on clause library update.
recommendation: Create regression test set: 100 representative submissions (mix of trial types: VLOS, BVLOS, autonomy). Before library deployment, run readiness check on test set with old library, then with new library. Fail deployment if pass-rate drops >5% (indicates rule tightening likely to impact production). If drop is acceptable (intentional tightening), generate summary: 'Pass rate dropped 10% due to ConOps clause tightening; expected impact 50 submissions/quarter.' Alert CAA communications team to prepare applicant guidance. Monitor production pass-rate for 1 week post-deployment; alert if drop exceeds predicted impact.

title: Missing ODD (Operational Design Domain) monitoring for swarm autonomy (input drift detection)
component: Onboard autonomy compute and ground-control state machine (NFR-MON-SWM-006, NFR-MON-SWM-007)
reasoning: REQ009 ML Assurance Case requires frozen-model manifest and explainability. Swarm autonomy model is trained on specific ODD (weather, airspace, aircraft type, swarm size). If trial operates outside trained ODD (e.g., weather unexpectedly changes, swarm expands beyond training size), onboard ML inference becomes unreliable (confidence <60%, or predictions diverge from expected behavior). Current monitoring tracks inference latency and confidence, but missing explicit ODD-boundary check: 'Are current operating conditions within trained ODD?'
recommendation: Instrument ODD monitoring: (1) define ODD bounds in model manifest (weather wind speed <15 mph, swarm size 5-10 vehicles, airspace type rural), (2) log operating conditions every 1 sec (wind speed, swarm size, airspace density), (3) compare to ODD bounds; alert if conditions exceed bounds. If ODD violation detected, trigger: (a) low-confidence inference flagging (confidence threshold raised to >80%), (b) ground-control alert: 'Operating outside trained ODD; recommend manual override,' (c) post-trial forensic report with ODD violations. Quarterly: measure ODD coverage across all trials (what % of trial-time was in/out of ODD); identify training-data gaps.

title: Missing confidence-threshold calibration for workbench AI inference (semantic classification and narrative generation)
component: Coverage matrix engine and AI-flag narrative service (NFR-MON-WB-003, NFR-MON-WB-004)
reasoning: Coverage matrix engine outputs confidence scores per evidence class (0-100%). Low-confidence classifications (40-60% range) should be flagged for manual review, but current monitoring only tracks aggregate confidence distribution. No threshold calibration: at what confidence level should system escalate to human reviewer? If threshold is too loose (>30%), legitimate low-confidence flags are not escalated; if too tight (<80%), system escalates everything (alert fatigue). As evidence distribution shifts with new trial types (autonomy vs swarm), confidence threshold needs adjustment.
recommendation: Implement confidence-threshold calibration: quarterly, measure precision/recall of classification at different thresholds (30%, 40%, 50%, 60%, 70%, 80%, 90%). Plot ROC curve: as threshold rises, recall drops but precision rises. Choose threshold at elbow (typically 60-70%): balances flagging genuinely ambiguous evidence vs over-escalating. If new trial type introduced (e.g., advanced ML swarm trials), retrain threshold on that cohort. Generate per-trial report: confidence histogram, % of classifications below threshold (escalation rate), regulator feedback on whether escalations were justified.

title: Missing applicant dashboard with readiness progression and ETA to gate pass
component: Applicant workspace and readiness check (NFR-USE-002, NFR-USE-003, NFR-USE-005)
reasoning: Parent story: 'Full workbench open-and-review cycle shall not require more than two working days.' Applicant experiences submission as long-running process: upload → matrix generation (10 min) → readiness check (1 min) → gate decision (days). Current dashboard shows current step only; applicant cannot see: time spent per step, remaining time to gate pass, which blocking issues are high-impact. High abandonment risk if applicant perceives progress is stuck (no feedback for 2+ hours during matrix generation). Monitoring collects telemetry; missing dashboard to visualize it for applicant perspective.
recommendation: Create applicant dashboard: (1) submission status (received, upload-in-progress, matrix-generating, readiness-checking, awaiting-panel), (2) progress timeline: matrix generation 5/10 min complete (50%), expected finish 10 min from now, (3) blocking issues (if any): 'ConOps document missing - estimate 30 min to fix,' (4) ETA to panel review: 'Your submission is 3rd in queue; expect panel review to start 2 working days from now.' Refresh dashboard every 10s during long operations. Use optimistic estimates (show expected completion time, not hard deadline) to avoid unrealistic expectations.

title: Missing inspector/regulator dashboard: my active cases + SLA countdowns + escalation queue
component: Inspector review sessions and panel deliberation (NFR-USE-007, NFR-USE-015)
reasoning: Regulators manage multiple cases in parallel during a session (panel, SORA review, post-decision verification). No unified view of: (1) my active cases and their status (waiting for evidence, awaiting my annotation, ready for panel), (2) SLA countdowns (this case must be decided in 5 days, clock started on submission date), (3) escalation queue (cases flagged by other inspectors as awaiting my input). Missing dashboard leads to context-switching overhead and SLA misses. Parent story: regulator must maintain panel productivity; missing visibility costs time.
recommendation: Create regulator dashboard: (1) 'My Active Cases' table: case ID, applicant, case status, my pending action, SLA countdown (color-coded: green >5 days, amber 2-5 days, red <2 days), (2) 'Escalation Queue': cases assigned to me for second opinion or complex review (count, priority), (3) 'Next Panel Session': when is my next panel, how many cases on agenda, any prep items due. Refresh every 5 min. Implement drill-down: click case → jump to case-open view (NFR-LAT-006). Add calendar integration: show panel session windows blocked on calendar.

title: Missing CAA Innovation Hub dashboard: portfolio view of calibration statistics and drift signals
reasoning: CAA Innovation Hub oversees AI-flag narrative service quality and regulator calibration. Key metrics: (1) inter-rater reliability (Cohen's kappa) across panel cohorts, (2) AI-flag override rate trends (is narrative becoming more trustworthy over time?), (3) input distribution drift (evidence types changing), (4) model confidence trends. Missing dashboard means Innovation Hub cannot answer: 'Are we improving AI reliability?' or 'Should we retrain the model?' Strategic decisions lack data visibility.
recommendation: Create Innovation Hub dashboard: (1) calibration scorecard: inter-rater kappa per panel, kappa trend over quarters (target >0.7), (2) AI-flag trustworthiness: override rate per regulator (histogram), trend over quarters, (3) evidence distribution: top-10 evidence types by frequency, % change month-over-month, (4) model drift: input distribution shift (KS test p-value), prediction distribution shift (flag rate trend), confidence trend. Refresh daily. Add drill-down: click panel → see kappa breakdown by case type (VLOS vs BVLOS). Generate quarterly report for leadership with key findings and recommendations (retrain model? recalibrate threshold?).

title: Missing MAA/MOD regulatory dashboard: swarm trial performance metrics and safety signals
component: Swarm operations and telemetry (NFR-MON-SWM-001 through SWM-007)
reasoning: MAA Regulatory Branch (MOD-aligned) must oversee approved swarm operations. Key visibility: (1) DAA encounter rate and resolution latency per trial, (2) C2 link quality (SNR, packet loss), (3) battery/contingency margins per swarm, (4) autonomous consensus health, (5) safety events (geofence breaches, C2 loss, sensor failures). Missing dashboard means MOD cannot rapidly assess trial safety posture or identify trends (e.g., 'C2 quality degrading over time' → suggest RF path optimization). REQ006 mandates MOD oversight; monitoring must support that.
recommendation: Create MOD regulatory dashboard (on-premises access): (1) active-trials map: swarm location, status (in-flight, landed, RTL), personnel (pilot, mission controller), (2) per-trial metrics: DAA encounter count + resolution latency, C2 SNR/loss-rate, battery state of charge (per vehicle), geofence compliance, (3) safety-event log: any geofence breach, C2 loss >10s, sensor failure (last 24h), (4) trends: encounter rate per flight hour, C2 link quality correlation with location (identifies bad RF zones). Real-time updates every 5s during active trials. Incident escalation: if any safety event detected, alert MOD ops (red banner, SMS).

title: Missing DDH/RAISO accountability dashboard: authority exercised + decisions made per regulator
component: Audit trail and decision recording (NFR-MON-WB-007, NFR-AVL-W006)
reasoning: DDH (Deputy Director, Horizon) and RAISO (Regulatory Authority Swarm Inspection Operator) must maintain regulatory accountability. Dashboard tracks: (1) which regulator made which decision (case X approved by regulator Y on date Z), (2) decision reversals (case initially approved, later revoked due to new evidence), (3) anomalies (regulator X approves cases at 10% override rate, regulator Y at 70% → investigate bias), (4) decision consistency (similar cases decided differently by different regulators → train cadre). Missing dashboard hides accountability and decision quality issues.
recommendation: Create DDH/RAISO accountability dashboard (CAA officers only): (1) decision log: case ID, regulator, decision (APPROVED/CONDITIONAL/REJECTED), decision date, conditions imposed, (2) decision-maker activity: regulator ID, cases decided this quarter, average decision time, override rate on AI flags, (3) anomaly detection: regulator X has unusual decision pattern (approve rate > 2 std dev from cohort mean) → flag for leadership review, (4) reversals: any decision reversed post-approval (new evidence, appeal) → track and analyze root cause. Refresh daily. Generate monthly report: decision quality metrics, any regulators needing additional training, appeals/reversals analysis.
